title: Massage Therapist
metaTitle: Massage Therapist Resume: Tips & Examples for 2025
metaDescription: Write a standout massage therapist resume with real examples, key skills, and ATS keywords. Land more client and clinic interviews.
h1: Massage Therapist Resume: How to Land More Bookings and Job Offers
intro: Your massage therapy resume needs to do more than list certifications—it needs to show clinics and spas that you're a skilled, professional practitioner clients will trust. We'll walk you through the exact skills, bullets, and structure that get results.
audience: Licensed massage therapists seeking positions at spas, clinics, or wellness centers; career switchers from healthcare or fitness; and recent graduates of massage therapy programs.

topSkills.name: Swedish Massage
topSkills.why: The most common modality employers and clients request; demonstrating competency here is table stakes.

topSkills.name: Deep Tissue Massage
topSkills.why: Shows ability to work with muscle tension and pain management—a high-value service clinics market heavily.

topSkills.name: Client Assessment & Intake
topSkills.why: Hiring managers want therapists who can safely screen for contraindications and customize treatment.

topSkills.name: Therapeutic Modalities (e.g., Sports Massage, Trigger Point, Myofascial Release)
topSkills.why: Specialty skills justify premium pricing and set you apart from generic relaxation-only therapists.

topSkills.name: HIPAA Compliance & Confidentiality
topSkills.why: Clinics and medical spas require absolute trust; listing this signals professionalism and legal awareness.

topSkills.name: Client Retention & Booking Management
topSkills.why: Spas care about repeat business; showing you can build loyal clientele proves business-side value.

topSkills.name: Anatomy & Physiology Knowledge
topSkills.why: Demonstrates competency in the science behind your work—essential for clinical and medical spa roles.

topSkills.name: Ergonomics & Body Mechanics
topSkills.why: Employers want therapists who can work long hours without injury; this signals sustainability and safety awareness.

topSkills.name: Licensed Massage Therapist (LMT) Certification
topSkills.why: Non-negotiable credential in most states; must be clearly stated with your license number and state.

sampleBullets.weak: Provided massage therapy services to clients and helped them feel better.
sampleBullets.strong: Delivered customized Swedish, deep tissue, and trigger point massage to 25–35 weekly clients; maintained 85%+ rebook rate through personalized treatment plans and follow-up care.
sampleBullets.lesson: Specific modalities, client volume, and retention metrics prove you're results-driven and organized.

sampleBullets.weak: Worked at a busy spa and did various types of massages.
sampleBullets.strong: Managed peak-season client load at full-service spa (50+ appointments/week); reduced no-show rate by 15% via automated appointment reminders and same-day confirmation calls.
sampleBullets.lesson: Quantified your impact on operations and client behavior—spas need therapists who help the business run smoother.

sampleBullets.weak: Educated clients about relaxation and wellness.
sampleBullets.strong: Developed and delivered 8-week chair massage wellness program at corporate client site (120+ employees); generated $2K+ in referral revenue and led to 3 additional corporate contracts.
sampleBullets.lesson: Show revenue, scope (number of people), and repeat business—this proves you can be a revenue driver, not just a service provider.

commonMistakes.mistake: Forgetting to list your license number and state clearly.
commonMistakes.fix: Add a dedicated 'Licenses & Certifications' section with full state abbreviation, license number, and expiration date—clinics screen for this immediately.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using vague language like 'helped clients relax' for every bullet.
commonMistakes.fix: Swap generic wellness language for specific modalities, client volume, retention rates, and any business impact (bookings, referrals, revenue).

commonMistakes.mistake: Listing every certification but no context on how you use them.
commonMistakes.fix: Show which certifications directly support your strongest skills; if you're a sports massage specialist, lead with that, not just 'massage therapy certified.'

commonMistakes.mistake: Omitting client management or business-side achievements.
commonMistakes.fix: Spas and clinics are businesses—mention if you built a loyal clientele, reduced cancellations, or drummed up referrals.

commonMistakes.mistake: Not emphasizing safety, hygiene, or compliance practices.
commonMistakes.fix: Call out HIPAA compliance, sanitation protocols, or injury-prevention measures to signal you're professional and trustworthy.

structureTips: Lead with a 'Licenses & Certifications' section at the top; clinics scan for this first and may auto-disqualify if it's buried.
Group experience by modality or setting (e.g., 'Sports Massage Therapist at XYZ Clinic' and 'Spa Massage Therapist at ABC Resort') to show specialization and versatility.
Place your strongest, most quantified achievement bullets in your most recent role—hiring managers spend the most time reading the top job.

Use a separate 'Core Competencies' section to mirror the exact modalities and skills listed in the job posting (e.g., 'Therapeutic Modalities: Swedish, Deep Tissue, Myofascial Release, Trigger Point').
salarySignal: Entry-level massage therapists in the US typically earn $28K–$38K annually, while experienced therapists in spas and wellness clinics range from $35K–$55K; independent contractors and those in high-demand specialties (sports, medical) often earn more.

faq.q: Do I need to list my massage license number on my resume?
faq.a: Yes—always include your full license number, state, and expiration date in your 'Licenses & Certifications' section. Clinics and spas verify this immediately, so missing or incorrect info can disqualify you.

faq.q: What's the best way to show my specialty (e.g., sports massage or prenatal)?
faq.a: Lead your job titles or a dedicated 'Core Competencies' section with your specialty. For example, write 'Sports Massage Therapist – XYZ Clinic' instead of just 'Massage Therapist.' Use exact modality names from the job posting.

faq.q: Should I include my client testimonials or reviews on my resume?
faq.a: No—keep your resume professional and metric-driven. Instead, mention strong client retention rates, rebook percentages, or referral numbers. Offer to share testimonials separately if the employer asks.

faq.q: How do I quantify my massage therapy work if I'm self-employed or worked at a small practice?
faq.a: Track and list: weekly/monthly client volume, rebook or retention rate (%), referral revenue, specialties offered, and any corporate or wellness contracts. Even rough estimates (e.g., '20–30 clients/week') strengthen your bullets.

faq.q: What if I don't have formal work experience yet (e.g., recent grad)?
faq.a: Emphasize certifications, internships, clinical rotations, and practicum hours. Add a 'Core Competencies' section listing all modalities you've trained in, and include any volunteer or student clinic experience with client counts and modalities used.
